They begin as murmurs at river crossings, then chalk on fenceposts, then a thread of weather braided through breath and memory. Credible displays of faith appear as plain sight: a craftsman mends a boat after a storm, a child teaches a stranger how to cross a plaza, rain arriving when neighbors join hands. Group movement traces rivers, rails, and roads, copying rites to endure, while bystanders imitate to survive. Beliefs rise as by-products of hunger and danger, tempered by schooling and memory. In time, weather of choice endures, steering memory toward mercy and a kinder tomorrow for all living beings everywhere, together, always onward.
